answer: Harvard University was founded on October 28, 1636, by the Massachusetts legislative assembly. John Harvard, though not the founder, was the first major benefactor, donating his estate and library. Harvard College was renamed in his honor in 1639.

results.title: This Week in History: Harvard University was founded in 1636
results.snippet: # This Week in History: Harvard University was founded in 1636. Harvard College (now Harvard University) founded, 1636 (Oct. 28). Harvard University was founded at Newtowne (now Cambridge), Massachusetts, on Oct. 28, 1636, just 16 years after the Pilgrims landed at Plymouth. The Massachusetts legislative assembly initially founded Harvard as New College, a school for educating clergymen. The school’s name was changed to Harvard College in 1639, when it was named after the school’s first benefactor, John Harvard. Harvard College opened that same year. Today, a statue of John Harvard stands in Harvard Yard in front of University Hall. More than 250 years after the fire, Harvard now houses the world’s largest university library system. Harvard College was officially renamed Harvard University in 1782 after the founding of its medical school. These include Wadsworth House, which General George Washington used as his first headquarters, and Lowell Hall, named after former Harvard President Abbott Lawrence Lowell.

results.title: Harvard University Is Founded, 1636 - Landmark Events
results.snippet: # Harvard University Is Founded, 1636. Only in Emmanuel College at Cambridge University were Puritan worthies fully in charge of higher English education, and it should be noted that they sent more graduates to New England than any other English college. John Harvard himself was a Puritan minister in possession of a Masters of Arts degree from Emmanuel College at Cambridge University. John Harvard (1607–1638), Puritan clergyman and first benefactor of Harvard University. results.title: This Week in Church History - Founding of Harvard College (1636)
results.snippet: # This Week in Church History: Founding of Harvard College (1636). September 8, 1636 marks the founding of Harvard College in what was then called New Towne, but would come to be known as Cambridge, MA in later years. The college was originally founded as New College but was renamed two years after opening in honor of one of the school’s first benefactors, the Rev. John Harvard. Upon his death, Harvard left his library of several hundred books and half of his estate, roughly £800, to the college. In honor of his generosity, the school was renamed Harvard College. The donation of Harvard’s library and estate to the college put the school on firmer ground and also encouraged others to donate to the fledgling school. His son Cotton, who was also grandson to John Cotton as Cotton’s daughter had married Increase, would complete a BA at Harvard in 1678 and an MA in 1681.

results.title: European Custom Causes Indefinite Date of 1636 for Harvard Founding | News | The Harvard Crimson
results.snippet: For in 1636 there was not a building, a teacher, or a pupil to which the historians might point as the beginning of the University that celebrates September 3, 1636 as the date of its origin. Indeed, it seemed reasonable to believe that no academic course of any kind began until 1638, and it was this year that Pierce in his "History of Harvard University" said that "historians fix as the date of the foundation of the college." Early almanacs were accustomed to place the foundation in the year 1642. In a footnote to his history of the University now in preparation, he says, "Colleges of the British Isles and Latin America date their foundation from the first charter, papal bull or formal act authorizing the institution; for it is nearly impossible to ascertain the dates of breaking ground for the first building or of first receiving scholars.". With surprising logic, the committee appointed in 1835 observed that the Massachusetts General Court must have begun its regular fall session before October 28, the day on which they issued the order for 400 pounds.
